title: Detection of Multidrug-Resistant Staphylococcus Aureus and Associated Genes Isolated from Hospital Surfaces in Mardan
author: Afzaal, Rahim
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description: The most important opportunistic pathogen that causes nosocomial infections and take part in substantial morbidity and mortality is  Staphylococcus aureus.  The study is aimed to determine the frequency of multidrug resistant  S. aureus  on hospital surfaces, and the level of resistance posed by  S. aureus  isolated from surfaces to empirically used antibiotics. Sixty samples from various hospital surfaces in the medicine ward in Mardan medical complex, Mardan were collected, and were transported to microbiology lab, Abdul Wali Khan University Mardan for further analysis. All samples were cultured on mannitol salt agar (MSA) and were incubated at 37ºC for 24 hours.  All bacteria were identified on biochemical tests. Bacteria were grown on  Mueller Hinton agar (MHA), and a ntibiotic susceptibility tests (AST) were  performed using the standard Kirby-Bauer disk diffusion method. Antibiotics belonging to 08 classes of antibiotics were used for anti-biogram analysis. Deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) from all  S. aureus  isolates was extracted for associated resistance genes analysis. Specific primers were used for amplification of resistance genes on polymerase chain reaction (PCR). Among the total of 60 samples, 28 were positive for  S.aureus . It was confirmed through PCR that 46 % of  S. aureus  isolates were  mec A positive and 39% were  erm C positive. The study shows that hospital environment is one of the richest environments for dissemination of resistant bacteria which could lead to severe infections. It is concluded that hospital staff need to follow standard operating procedures and should be very cautious on touching hospital surfaces to contain the spread of nosocomial infections. Moreover, a proper protocol should be devised for all those visitors attending patients inside hospital.

keywords: Staphylococcus Aureus, Multidrug resistance, Antibiotics, Hospital surfaces, Antibiotic susceptibility tests.
